meta.title: 1 Series Engines — The Complete UK Guide

hero.tagPill: BMW 1 Series • 2004–Present • 3 Generations (E81/E87, F20, F40) • N47, B47, N52, B48
hero.h1: 1 Series Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every BMW 1 Series engine across three generations — from the N47's rear-chain tensioner to the B47's revised architecture. Real UK replacement costs, known failure patterns, and honest ownership economics for every E87 to F40. Backed by 762+ real UK 1 Series engine enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED].

metrics.ourCall: ⚠️ The 1 Series rule — the facelift is the line: a pre-2016 N47 recon at £3,200–£5,500 against a £3,500–£5,000 E87/F20 can exceed 70% of the car's value; post-2016 B47/B48 cars at £7,000+ absorb it comfortably.
★★★★☆ — one model, two distinct lives: pre-2016 N47s carry the chain caveat; 2016+ B47/B48 cars are significantly stronger.
Pre-2016 N47D20C without documented timing-chain replacement
2016+ F20 LCI with B47D20A diesel or B48 petrol — post-chain-revision, modern electronics, entry-level BMW ownership done right
F40 118i with B48B20 — the four-cylinder at its most refined in a compact hatch
N47D20C timing chain stretch (1,450 JLR-wide; 344 F20 model-level) [BMW-VERIFIED]

ownershipVerdict.oneLineVerdict: The 1 Series is BMW's gateway car and the second most-enquired model in our data — not because the engines are uniquely bad, but because there are so many of them and the pre-2016 timing chain question hangs over every used listing. Buy on the facelift line, not the badge.

generations.h2: BMW 1 Series Generations — Find Your Vehicle
generations.subHeadline: Three generations, one entry-level formula that grew from compact coupe to premium hatchback. The right 1 Series depends entirely on which generation and engine you're willing to live with.

cards.title: BMW 1 Series E81/E82/E87/E88
cards.verdict: The original 1 Series — and the N47's rear-chain tensioner made it a used-market minefield. A 130i with the N52 straight-six is the petrol sweet spot; any diesel without a documented chain replacement is a gamble.

cards.title: BMW 1 Series F20/F21
cards.verdict: The generation where BMW quietly fixed the chain issue (LCI 2016+) and refined the four-cylinder to near-perfection. Pre-2016 N47 cars carry the E87's risk; 2016+ B47/B58 cars are the 1 Series at its most sensible — and the M140i with the B58 is a future classic.

cards.title: BMW 1 Series F40
cards.verdict: The front-wheel-drive 1 Series that purists love to hate — but the B-family engines are the safest ever fitted to the badge. The M135i's B58 is as robust as it is rapid; the diesels are the most dependable entry-level BMWs on sale.

engineDatabase.h2: BMW 1 Series Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine ever fitted to a BMW 1 Series — verified codes, specifications and real UK ownership data.

engines.enquiries: — (pattern data — under B58 aggregate in source data)

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: N47D20C rating: 95% confidence — 1,450 JLR-wide enquiries [BMW-VERIFIED] plus 344 model-level F20 enquiries, cross-referenced with BMW TSB data and 100+ specialist interviews. B47D20A and B58B30 ratings: 90% confidence — high-volume modern engines with clear failure patterns. N52 and N55 ratings: Expert-knowledge tier — documented patterns and specialist rebuild data. Early E87 petrols: Pattern-data tier — documented by enthusiast records and specialist consensus, not proprietary volume data. Data note (internal) — corrections applied: Correction applied: The source data listed "N47D20" as a single engine. Verified reality is that the E87 received the N47D20C (2004–2011, 143–177 bhp), while the F20 LCI (2016+) received the B47D20A. The N47D20C is the high-risk chain-tensioner engine; the B47D20A is the revised front-chain architecture. The source data must be split to distinguish pre-2016 from 2016+ variants. Correction applied: The N54B30 was listed with no failure pattern for the 1 Series. Verified: high-pressure fuel pump (HPFP) failures are the defining N54 issue, not a timing chain problem. Updated the Known Failure Patterns section accordingly. Correction applied: "B48B20" engine was aggregated under "B58" in the source data's enquiry counts. They are separate — B48 is the four-cylinder, B58 is the six. Split accordingly. B58B30 has real enquiry data (540); B48B20 is pattern-data-tier until the source file can separate them.

commonProblems.subHeadline: The six most common failure patterns across three generations — symptoms, costs and urgency.

problems.description: The defining 1 Series issue and our #1 enquiry driver on this model [BMW-VERIFIED]. Cold-start rattle from the rear of the engine is the warning; ignored, chain slip means a full engine replacement. Affects E87 and pre-2016 F20. £3,200–£5,500 recon [BMW-QUOTE].

The twin-turbo 135i's Achilles heel — high-pressure fuel pump failure leads to long cranks and limp mode; injector issues follow. Affects E87 135i (2008–2010). BMW extended warranty covered many; check history before buying.
Not an engine failure — but a common non-engine 1 Series enquiry. Rear subframe mount cracking on high-mileage E87 cars. Check on any 1 Series over 80k miles.
The early E87 petrol's known issues — Valvetronic eccentric shaft sensor failure and coil pack degradation. Repairable, moderate cost, well-understood by any BMW specialist.
The modern straight-six's known weak points — coolant expansion tank leaks and PCV valve failures. Repairs are £400–£800, not catastrophic if caught early. Affects F20 LCI and F40 M135i.
The B47's primary weak points — EGR cooler leaks and DPF clogging on urban-driven cars. Well-documented, repairable, and far less severe than the N47's chain issue.

marketIntelligence.h2: BMW 1 Series Market Intelligence — What 762 UK Owners Told Us in 2025

signals.demandTrend: ⬆ Rising as the parc exits warranty cover [PLACEHOLDER — YoY %]

insights: Interesting insight: The 1 Series is the second most-enquired BMW model in our dataset — 762 enquiries across three generations — but the split tells the story: the F20 (344) dominates, followed by the E87 (240), with the F40 (158) showing that newer cars generate due-diligence searches, not failure enquiries. The N47's chain question is still driving demand years after production ended. (Editorial synthesis — specialist sense-check before publishing.)

E87 enquiries are concentrated on the 2007–2012 diesel parc now exiting affordable-warranty age — exactly the cars where a £3,200–£5,500 engine decision meets a £2,500–£4,500 vehicle. The 70% threshold is live on this generation; on a tired E87 diesel, we'll honestly tell you to walk away. (Editorial synthesis.)

Petrol enquiries (N46, N52, N55, B58) remain minimal compared to diesel — consistent with the smaller petrol parc on UK roads, not evidence of superior reliability alone. The B58's 540 enquiries (JLR-wide) are largely from M140i/M135i owners doing due diligence, not responding to failures. (Editorial synthesis.)

editorialPullQuote.title: The One Thing We'd Tell Every BMW 1 Series Buyer
editorialPullQuote.quote: If you buy one 1 Series based on this page, buy a 2016+ F20 LCI with the B47 diesel or B48 petrol. The LCI brought the revised chain hardware that changed the diesel's reliability story — and on an E87 or pre-LCI F20 N47, a documented timing-chain inspection is worth more than any service stamp.

replacementCosts.h2: BMW 1 Series Engine Replacement Costs — By Generation
replacementCosts.subHeadline: Real UK pricing for used, reconditioned and rebuilt engines — supply only, with labour guidance.
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Labour estimate: Fitting a BMW 1 Series engine typically takes 8–14 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [BMW-QUOTE]. The 1 Series rule — the facelift is the line: A £3,200–£5,500 N47 recon against a £4,500 E87 or pre-2016 F20 sits at 70–122% of the car's value. On an older sub-£4k car, that exceeds our 70% BMW threshold — we'll honestly tell you to walk away. Against a £12,000 F20 LCI or a £20,000 F40, the same recon is just 27–46% of the car's value — a routine repair. Buy on the facelift line; the engine decision follows.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a BMW 1 Series engine typically takes 8–14 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [BMW-QUOTE]. On an older sub-£4k car, that exceeds our 70% BMW threshold — we'll honestly tell you to walk away. Against a £12,000 F20 LCI or a £20,000 F40, the same recon is just 27–46% of the car's value — a routine repair. Buy on the facelift line; the engine decision follows.

engineEvolution.h2: How the BMW 1 Series Engine Range Evolved — 2004 to Today

eras.era: Launch era — E87 petrols & N47 arrival
eras.whyBmwChanged: BMW's entry-level model launched with four and six-cylinder petrols; the N47 diesel arrived in 2007 as the fuel-efficient option
eras.worthKnowing: The N47's rear-mounted timing chain defined this era's reputation — every used E87 diesel lives in its shadow

eras.whyBmwChanged: The second-generation 1 Series brought improved handling and the N55 turbo straight-six to the M135i
eras.worthKnowing: The N55 is robust but the N47 chain question continued; 2016 LCI was the real fix

eras.whyBmwChanged: The LCI quietly replaced the N47 with the B47 and introduced the B58 to the M140i — the reliability story changed more than the styling
eras.worthKnowing: The B58 M140i is now a future classic; the B47 diesel finally fixed the chain issue that defined the model

eras.whyBmwChanged: The move to the FAAR front-wheel-drive platform and full B-family engine adoption; emissions and efficiency drove the change
eras.worthKnowing: The B-family engines are the safest ever fitted to a 1 Series; purists lament the drive layout, but the powertrains are genuinely strong

whoShouldBuy.h2: Who Should Buy a BMW 1 Series?

profiles.verdict: The 1 Series hatchback is a capable small family car but the 3 Series Touring does the job better. For occasional rear-seat use and a 350-litre boot, the F20/F40 works; for regular family hauling, size up.

This is what the 1 Series does best. 30 miles a day in town? The B47 diesel or B48 petrol is perfect. Urban commuting? The F40's front-wheel-drive and modern diesel emissions make it the ideal city BMW.
A cheap E87 N47 is not a cheap 1 Series — it's an expensive one with the first year's bills prepaid by nobody. A £5,000 F20 with chain paperwork is the bargain; a £3,000 E87 without it is the trap.

The M140i with the B58 is one of the greatest hot hatches ever sold in Britain — 340 bhp, rear-wheel drive, and a straight-six that sings. The F40 M135i xDrive is faster but less characterful; buy the F20 LCI M140i while values are still sensible.

The 1 Series is BMW's gateway car — and the F40 with the B48 or B47 is the safest entry point into premium ownership. Approachable running costs, modern tech, and a warranty if you buy approved used. Start post-2016 and it's a genuinely easy ownership entry.

calculatorCta.intro: Cold-start rattle on an N47? Chain noise on a high-mileage F20? On the 1 Series, the answer swings on your car's value — an E87 at £4,500 and an F40 at £22,000 face very different economics. Get a personalised repair-vs-replace verdict using real UK cost data.

items.question: How much does a BMW 1 Series engine replacement cost in the UK?
items.answer: A reconditioned BMW 1 Series engine typically costs £2,800–£5,500 supply only [BMW-QUOTE], with the volume N47D20C diesel at £3,200–£5,500 and the B58B30 petrol at £3,800–£6,000. Fitting adds £650–£1,500. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

items.question: Which BMW 1 Series engine is the most reliable?
items.answer: The B48B20 petrol and post-2016 B47D20A diesel. The 2016 F20 LCI's revised chain hardware materially changed the diesel's reliability profile; the B48's direct-injection architecture is the most refined four-cylinder BMW has made.

items.question: Which BMW 1 Series engine should I avoid?
items.answer: No engine outright — but a pre-2016 N47D20C without a documented timing-chain replacement is the one purchase we consistently advise against. The engine itself is solid; the rear-mounted tensioner design is the problem. A 2016+ B47 or any B58 has no such caveat.

items.question: How do I know if my N47 timing chain is worn?
items.answer: Cold-start rattle for 1–2 seconds is the classic early warning, sometimes with a chain-noise change under load. On the N54 135i, look for long cranking and HPFP failure. On the B58, coolant loss from the expansion tank is the early signal. Caught early, it's a repair; ignored, it's a £3,200–£5,500 engine [BMW-QUOTE]. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

items.question: Is it worth replacing a BMW 1 Series engine?
items.answer: Depends entirely on generation. Against a £22,000 F40, a £5,000 B58 recon is just 23% of the car's value — ✅ safe. Against a £3,500 E87, a £4,000 N47 recon is 114% — ❌ walk away. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line: below 70% is safe; above it, run the maths carefully. Use our calculator for your car.

items.question: Which BMW 1 Series generation should I buy?
items.answer: 2016+ F20 LCI with the B47 diesel or B48 petrol. You get the facelift architecture, the revised chain hardware, and the electronics update — plus the M140i with the B58 is a future classic that still depreciates. For the safest entry, an F40 118i with B48 is the most dependable 1 Series ever made.

items.question: How do I find my BMW 1 Series engine code?
items.answer: Listed on the VIN plate and in the vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible from below on most N47/B47 units, and on the cylinder head on N52/B58 engines. Or tell us your model year and variant (118d, 120i, M135i) and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted specialists will confirm it for free.

items.question: BMW 1 Series vs 3 Series — which is more reliable?
items.answer: Mechanically they share engines across the same eras — the N47/B47/B58 story is identical. The 1 Series is lighter, so it works the powertrain less hard; the 3 Series adds weight but benefits from the same engineering. Buy on engine and year, not badge. Read the full head-to-head →
